title: Why Your Shower Has Better Ideas Than Your Desk (A Neuroscience Investigation)
excerpt: Your brain's best work happens when you stop trying. Here's why, and what AI diffusion models accidentally proved about creativity.

title: The Fermi Paradox But For Good Software (Where Is Everybody?)
excerpt: If we have millions of developers and decades of practice, why does most software still suck? Apply Fermi paradox logic.

title: Every New Year's Resolution Is a Negotiation With a Stranger
excerpt: Your future self is basically a different person and you're making promises on their behalf. On temporal discounting and doing RLHF on yourself.

title: The Dunning-Kruger Peak Is Where All the Confidence Lives
excerpt: The sweet spot where you know just enough to be dangerously wrong — and why AI models go through the exact same thing during training.

title: Your Phone's Battery Percentage Is a Metaphor for Everything
excerpt: How we manage phone battery maps perfectly onto how we manage energy, attention, motivation, and relationships.

title: The Turing Test Was Always the Wrong Test (And Alan Would Probably Agree)
excerpt: Why fooling a human into thinking a machine is human is a terrible benchmark for intelligence. What we should actually measure.

title: Why Every Meeting Could Have Been an Email (But Also Shouldn't Have Been)
excerpt: The bandwidth problem in communication. Meetings are TCP with massive overhead, emails are UDP, and Slack is lossy compression.

title: The Algorithm Doesn't Know You. It Knows a Puppet That Looks Like You.
excerpt: Recommendation algorithms don't model the real you — they model a simplified statistical puppet built from your clicks.

title: Sleep Is Just Your Brain's Garbage Collection Day
excerpt: Your brain literally pressure-washes itself every night. Dreams are just the test suite running edge cases. Skip it at your own risk.

title: The 10,000 Hour Rule Is a Lie (But the Truth Is Weirder)
excerpt: Malcolm Gladwell misquoted the research. The truth about expertise is weirder, more nuanced, and maps perfectly to how we train AI.

title: Why We're All Running Someone Else's Code
excerpt: Your beliefs, habits, and identity are programs you didn't write, installed by people you didn't choose. Sound familiar? So is AI.

title: Love Is Just Two Reward Functions That Accidentally Aligned (A Valentine's Day Special)
excerpt: Oxytocin, dopamine, serotonin, and the alignment problem — in relationships and in AI. A Valentine's Day investigation.

title: The Trolley Problem Is Easy. The Real Problem Is the Pedestrian Detection.
excerpt: Everyone debates the trolley problem for self-driving cars. The actual hard problem is way more boring and way more important.

title: Why You Can't Remember Where You Put Your Keys But Can Quote Entire Movie Scenes
excerpt: Memory encoding isn't about importance — it's about emotional weight, narrative structure, and repetition. Your brain is a terrible filing clerk.

title: The Map Is Not the Territory But Google Maps Is Getting Dangerously Close
excerpt: On models, reality, and the strange loop where Google Maps reshapes the world it claims to merely describe.

title: Imposter Syndrome Is Just Your Brain Running Antivirus on Your Confidence
excerpt: Why high performers doubt themselves, why incompetent people don't, and what AI hallucinations have to do with all of it.

title: The Attention Economy Is a Ponzi Scheme (And We're All Invested)
excerpt: Attention is the new currency, but the supply is fixed at ~16 waking hours and every app wants more. The maths doesn't work.

title: Why Your Smart Home Is Actually a Dumb Home With Good Marketing
excerpt: The reality of 'smart' home devices: connected, not intelligent. The light bulb that needs a firmware update. The fridge that tweets.

title: Your Brain Has Two Employees and Neither of Them Have Read the Job Description
excerpt: Everyone procrastinates. Everyone knows they shouldn't. And yet here we are, 45 minutes deep into whether octopuses dream. The weird thing? AI has exactly the same problem.

title: How Pokemon Go Players Accidentally Built the World's Best Robot Navigation System
excerpt: For ten years, millions walked around catching imaginary creatures. Turns out they were also training the most sophisticated pedestrian navigation dataset ever created.
